ACKNOWLEDGMENT
First of all, I would like to express my appreciation to God, who gave me the strength and wisdom to complete this final year project. I am also very thankful to my supervisor Mr Fredrick Aminu Nantogmah, who is a lecturer in the School of Computing and Information Sciences, for his encouragement and guidance throughout the project. I appreciate the support and teachings of all my classmates and other lecturers, who helped me in the requirement gathering for this project and to develop my skills, knowledge and attitude towards achieving the objectives of this project. Finally, I would like to thank my parents and families for supporting my studies and giving me valuable suggestions and feedback to improve the outcome of the project.
ABSTRACT
This report elaborates on the chosen research, development of online learning management system for schools. The LMS developed offers a platform for sharing of learning materials, scheduling classes, taking assignments and more. It enhances communication and collaborations among students and their instructors. The LMS can be used within any level of education as it focuses on adaptability. The agile methodology was used in building the system. 
The project was guided by key findings which show the impact of technology on education. By using frameworks such as the TAM and UTAUT, the project develops a cost-effective, adaptable LMS. Insights from social presence theory and CoI emphasized effective communication and collaboration in education.
Future recommendations include improvement, testing, support, integration, and research on technology and education. The LMS presented through this project will help empower educational institutions to embrace online teaching and learning. The use of technology in education keeps growing and this work can serve as an essential step toward effective learning environments that benefit both teachers and students.

CHAPTER ONE
INTRODUCTION
Background of Study
In many universities around the world, including Ghana, access to educational materials outside the traditional classroom remains a challenge for many students. Traditional classroom-based education continues to be the predominant mode of instruction in the country (Darkwa & Antwi, 2021). The advancement of emerging technologies presents opportunities to transform traditional education, which has been traditionally associated with high costs and rigid structures in physical classrooms (Mhlongo et al., 2023). Internet connectivity plays a crucial role in the transition from face-to-face teaching to online courses. 
Technology complements teachers rather than replacing them, as educators continue to play a central role in facilitating online classrooms and delivering course content. Moreover, this shift towards online education promotes accessibility and removes geographical barriers, allowing teaching and learning to take place in a more widespread and inclusive environment (Mhlongo et al., 2023).
LMSs have emerged as a technological solution that has gained traction in recent years, particularly in the wake of the COVID-19 pandemic (Raza et al., 2021). LMS platforms offer a centralized hub for students and lecturers to access course materials, communicate with each other, and collaborate on assignments and quizzes (Turnbull et al., 2020a). These systems have the potential to enhance student engagement, improve learning outcomes, and alleviate the administrative burden on lecturers and school administrators.
 LMS solution have shown to help improve student engagement and learning outcomes, and help to reduce the administrative burden on lecturers and school administrators (Steindal et al., 2021). They have potential benefits in enhancing the quality of education. One of the benefits of using an LMS in education is that it makes school administration easy. Another benefit is that every learning material can be collected in one place. You may now access them from a single platform instead of holding separate records and learning resources for students and other vital information
Despite the potential benefits of LMS, many schools continue to rely on traditional teaching methods due to various challenges, including a lack of resources and expertise in implementing and managing such systems (Cavus et al., 2021). This hinders the adoption of LMS platforms and limits the opportunities for students to access educational resources and engage in effective communication and collaboration. These issues make it difficult for most institutions to customize these systems based on the goals they want to achieve.
In this context, there is a pressing need for a bespoke LMS solution that can be customized to the unique needs and requirements of individual schools. Such a tailored LMS solution would address the specific challenges faced by schools in Ghana and provide a platform that supports the delivery of education while promoting collaboration and assessment between lecturers and students.
1.2 Problem Statement 
Despite the potential benefits of LMS in enhancing the quality of education, many schools in Ghana still rely on traditional face-to-face mode of teaching due to lack of resources and expertise in implementing and managing such systems.  
Objectives
The primary objective of this study is to develop a customized LMS that caters to the specific needs of schools in Ghana. The LMS will be designed to support the delivery of education and provide students with access to a wide range of educational resources. Additionally, the system will facilitate effective communication and collaboration between students and lecturers, ultimately aiming to improve learning outcomes and enhance the competitiveness of Ghanaian schools in the education sector.
1.2.1 Main Objectives
The main objective of this project is to design and develop a customized LMS for tertiary institutions which provides a platform for lecturers and students to access educational resources and collaborate effectively.
1.2.2 Specific Objectives
To facilitate lecturer-student-engagement online through a bespoke online system
To provide students with access to educational resources online
To enhance effective communication and collaboration between students and lecturers
Research Questions
How can lecturers use technology to create an interactive and engaging online learning experience?
What are some of the effective ways to provide students with access to educational resources online?
How can instructors use technology to make educational resources more accessible
What are some of the effective ways for instructors to communicate with students in an online learning environment?
Research Design
This study deploys the Agile methodology for the design and development of the LMS, accompanied by a qualitative data collection approach for gathering and analysing user requirements. Agile methodology is an iterative and flexible approach to software development that prioritizes adaptability and collaboration throughout the project's lifecycle (Alsaqqa et al., 2020). The project will embrace a cyclic and interactive progression through various iterations of the Agile methodology.
This approach places a premium on continuous feedback, swift adaptations, and close communication, ensuring that the development process remains responsive to evolving needs. Each iteration will encompass design, development, testing, and user feedback, fostering a dynamic environment that minimizes the likelihood of significant disruptions or delays.
Regular interactions with the project supervisor and frequent evaluations will guarantee that the LMS aligns with stakeholder requirements and anticipations. By adopting the Agile methodology, this research project aims to craft an LMS solution that optimizes communication, collaboration, and learning outcomes within educational contexts.
Scope of Study
This project is limited to the design and development of a LMS which can be used by universities and colleges with features such as access to course materials, and tools for collaboration between lecturers and students. The project does not include any modifications or upgrades to the existing IT infrastructure of the school.
Project Feasibility
The study aims to develop a LMS that is technically feasible with low development costs and a clear need for implementation to enhance the quality of education and provide access to educational resources for students. The project will use web-based technologies and tools and involve close collaboration with the supervisor and other students to ensure that the LMS meets specific needs and requirements. The project is deemed technically, economically, and legally feasible, indicating that it has a good chance of successful development and implementation.
1.9 Structure of the Dissertation
This thesis is organized into five chapters, each focusing on a specific aspect of the research. Chapter 1 provides an introduction and background to the study. 
Chapter 2 presents a comprehensive review of the literature, including a conceptual review that explores the use of information technology in tertiary level education, criteria for evaluating an LMS, Online Educational Resources (OER) and their implications in teaching and learning, as well as a theoretical review that examines specific learning theories relevant to LMS implementation. 
Chapter 3 describes the research methodology, outlining the research design, data collection methods, and data analysis techniques. Chapter 4 presents the findings of the study, including the development process of the bespoke LMS and its evaluation. Finally, Chapter 5 concludes the thesis with a summary of the key findings, implications for practice, and recommendations for future research.

CHAPTER TWO
LITERATURE REVIEW
2.1 Introduction
As schools and universities continue to transition their academic activities to online delivery, demand for LMSs has substantially grown. LMS have become integral to the educational system worldwide after the emergence of the COVID-19 pandemic (Raza et al., 2021). 
A LMS is basically a piece of software that makes it possible to create, manage, distribute, and evaluate educational content, programs, or materials. Different authors have proposed various definitions of LMS based on their perspectives and objectives. For example, Muhtarom and colleagues, (2022) defined LMS as an Online learning system that aims to meet educational standards by utilizing information technology to distribute subject matter and enable collaboration between students and teachers. Similarly, Falana and colleagues (2021) described LMS as a web-based software application developed to create, manage, and deliver e-learning courses. 
Examples of prominent LMSs used in schools include Blackboard, Moodle, Desire2Learn, Google Classroom, Schoology, TalentLM, Canvas LMS, eCoach, A Tutor, Skillshare, LearnUpon, Edmodo (Al-Hunaiyyan et al., 2020). They enable collaboration between students and teachers, facilitate the delivery of accurate information, and enhance students' potential to study at their own pace while still following the teaching process (Bradley, 2020). 
However, a study by Al-Hunaiyyan and colleagues (2020) on the Prospects and Challenges of LMSs in higher education identified some barriers that can affect e-learning adoption of these systems. These barriers include but are not limited to students’ interdictions with these systems, complexity of the LMS interface and instructors’ and students’ readiness. With the aid of a bespoke web-based LMS, data exchange among a network of computers over the Internet is simple to accomplish. Most courses now incorporate electronic content, and administration and management rely on technology and computer networks (Kapur, 2019). 
By harnessing the potential of advancements in technology within educational systems, aligning the educational framework with the progress of modern society, and establishing and improving LMS solutions, the significance of electronic teaching and learning can be increased (Lockee, 2021). The development of an LMS for schools can enable teachers and students to have a seamless and enriching learning experience (Statti & Torres, 2020).
This chapter presents a thorough review of the literature, including a conceptual review that investigates the use of technology in tertiary education, criteria for assessing an LMS, Online Educational Resources (OER) and their implications in teaching and learning, as well as a theoretical review that investigates particular learning theories pertinent to LMS implementation.
2.2 Conceptual Review
2.2.1 Information technology in Tertiary level education
Information technology in education (ITE) is the use of digital devices, software, applications, networks, and data to support and enhance teaching and learning processes and outcomes (Szymkowiak et al., 2021). ITE can be classified into three main types: instructional technology, which focuses on the design and delivery of educational content; administrative technology, which supports the management and organisation of educational institutions; and learning technology, which enables and facilitates individual and collaborative learning activities (Ahshan, 2021).
ITE has various features, such as interactivity, multimedia, adaptability, accessibility, and scalability, that can offer benefits for both educators and learners, such as increased motivation, engagement, feedback, personalization, flexibility, and efficiency (Major & Francis, 2020). However, ITE also poses some challenges, such as technical issues, digital divide, cyberbullying, plagiarism, privacy, and security risks, that require careful consideration and mitigation (Bećirović, 2023).
2.2.2 Criteria for Evaluating an LMS
Evaluating an LMS is a process of determining the extent to which an LMS meets the needs and expectations of its users and stakeholders. The criteria and methods for evaluating an LMS may vary depending on the purpose, scope, and context of the evaluation. However, some common criteria that are often used to evaluate an LMS are: quality, which refers to the accuracy, reliability, and validity of the content and functionality of an LMS; usability, which refers to the ease of use and user-friendliness of an LMS; accessibility, which refers to the availability and compatibility of an LMS for different devices, browsers, and users; effectiveness, which refers to the impact and outcomes of an LMS on teaching and learning (Mehrolia et al., 2021). 
Some common methods that are often used to evaluate an LMS are: surveys, which collect quantitative and qualitative data from users and stakeholders; interviews, which elicit in-depth information and opinions from users and stakeholders; observations, which record user behaviour and interaction with an LMS; tests, which measure user performance and achievement with an LMS; analytics, which analyse user data and statistics from an LMS (Nguyen, 2021). However, there is a gap here on studies that specifically address the development of a cost-effective LMS tailored to the needs of individual schools.
2.2.3 OERs and Their Implications in Teaching and Learning
OERs are digital materials that are freely available for use in teaching and learning. OER can include various types of content, such as textbooks, articles, videos, audio files, images, animations, simulations, games, etc. as well as tools, such as software, applications, platforms, etc., that enable the creation, delivery, modification, and sharing of OER (Georgiadou, 2020). OER have implications for teaching and learning in terms of access, quality, Cost, innovation, and collaboration. OER can increase access to education by providing diverse and flexible learning opportunities for learners who may face barriers such as time, location, or affordability (Shukla et al., 2020). 
OER can also enhance the quality of education by offering up-to-date, relevant, and customised content and pedagogy that can meet the needs and interests of different learners. OER can also reduce the cost of education by eliminating or lowering the expenses of purchasing, producing, and distributing educational materials. OER can also foster innovation in education by encouraging the creation, adaptation, and improvement of OER by educators and learners. OER can also promote collaboration in education by facilitating the sharing, exchange, and reuse of OER by educators and learners across different contexts and cultures (Georgiadou, 2020).
2.2.4 Communication and Collaboration in Teaching and Learning
Communication and collaboration are essential skills and processes for effective teaching and learning in an educational setting. Communication is the exchange of information, ideas, opinions, and emotions between two or more parties using verbal or non-verbal means (Ciancarini et al., 2021). Collaboration is the joint effort of two or more parties to achieve a common goal or outcome using communication and coordination. Communication and collaboration can occur in different modes, such as synchronous or asynchronous, depending on whether the parties interact in real time or at different times (Chan et al., 2021). 
Communication and collaboration can also use different tools and platforms, such as email, chat, video conferencing, social media, wikis, blogs, etc., depending on the purpose, format, and audience of the interaction. Communication and collaboration can also follow different protocols, such as etiquette, norms, rules, and roles, depending on the context, culture, and expectations of the interaction (Newman & Ford, 2021). Communication and collaboration can have a positive impact on student engagement and achievement by enhancing motivation, participation, feedback, peer support, and social learning. However, communication and collaboration can also pose some challenges, such as misunderstanding, conflict, inequality, and distraction, that require careful management and resolution (Catalan, 2021).
2.3 Theoretical Review
2.3.1 Technology Acceptance Model (TAM)
Traditional classroom lessons do not offer an immediate learning environment, fast evaluations, or more engagement (Schmidt & Tang, 2020). In contrast, technology and digital educational resources fill this gap. Some of the advantages that these technologies provide are simply unmatched by the traditional methods of teaching. 
Several theories have been developed and tested to guide and evaluate the design utilization of information technology in higher level education. One of the most widely used theories is the TAM, which predicts the adoption and usage of an IT system based on two main factors: perceived usefulness and perceived ease of use (Mustafa & Garcia, 2021).
Perceived usefulness refers to the degree to which a user believes that using an IT system will enhance his or her performance or outcomes. Perceived ease of use refers to the degree to which a user believes that using an IT system will be free of effort or difficulty (Mustafa & Garcia, 2021). According to TAM, both factors influence the user's attitude toward using an IT system, which in turn affects the user's behavioural intention to use an IT system, which ultimately determines the user's actual usage behaviour.
2.3.2 Unified Theory of Acceptance and Use of Technology (UTAUT)
Another influential theory that applies to LMS design and implementation is the UTAUT, which extends TAM by incorporating four additional factors: social influence, facilitating conditions, performance expectancy, and effort expectancy (Buabeng-Andoh & Baah, 2020).
According to (Rakhmawati et al., 2020), social influence refers to the degree to which a user perceives that important others believe that he or she should use an IT system. Facilitating conditions refer to the degree to which a user perceives that the technical and organizational infrastructure supports the use of an IT system. Performance expectancy refers to the degree to which a user expects that using an IT system will help him or her achieve his or her goals or tasks. Effort expectancy refers to the degree to which a user expects that using an IT system will be easy or simple.
According to UTAUT, these four factors, along with gender, age, experience, and voluntariness of use, moderate the relationship between the user's behavioural intention and actual usage behaviour (Obienu & Amadin, 2021). In this context, there is still the need for further research on effective strategies for providing online educational resources that align with diverse learning styles and preferences
2.3.3 Enhancing Communication and Collaboration
Communication and collaboration are two essential aspects of learning at the tertiary level, as they enable students and lecturers to exchange information, ideas, feedback, and support, as well as to co-construct knowledge and understanding. IT can enhance communication and collaboration in tertiary level education by providing various tools and platforms, such as email, chat, forums, blogs, wikis, podcasts, videoconferencing, social media, and more. These tools and platforms can facilitate synchronous and asynchronous communication and collaboration, as well as formal and informal communication and collaboration (Bailey & Lee, 2020).
2.3.3.1 Social Presence Theory
Several theories have been proposed to explain how IT can enhance communication and collaboration in tertiary level education (Tuma, 2021). One of the most prominent theories is the social presence theory, which suggests that the degree to which a user feels socially and emotionally connected with others in an online environment affects his or her communication and collaboration behaviour and outcomes. According to (Kreijns et al., 2022), social presence is influenced by various factors, such as the medium of communication, the content of communication, the context of communication, and the characteristics of the communicators. According to social presence theory, higher levels of social presence led to higher levels of communication quality, satisfaction, trust, involvement, participation, and learning outcomes.
2.3.3.2 Community of Inquiry (CoI) Framework
Another important theory that relates to communication and collaboration in tertiary level education is the community of inquiry (CoI) framework. It proposes that effective online learning occurs in a community of inquiry that consists of three interrelated elements: cognitive presence, social presence, and teaching presence (Shea et al., 2022).
 According to (Guo et al., 2021), cognitive presence refers to the extent to which learners are able to construct meaning through sustained communication and reflection. Social presence refers to the extent to which learners are able to project themselves socially and emotionally in an online environment. Teaching presence refers to the extent to which instructors are able to design, facilitate, and direct the learning process. 
According to CoI framework, these three elements interact and influence each other to create a meaningful and worthwhile online learning experience. In this context, there is a classical literature gap on comprehensive investigations into the most effective methods to enhance communication and collaboration between students and lecturers in an educational setting.
2.4 Summary
LMS and the efficient application of information technology to improve education are both topics covered in depth in this literature review. 
The conceptual review, which divides ITE into instructional, administrative, and learning technologies, emphasizes the transformative effects of ITE in education. 
The conceptual study also highlights the benefits of using OER for accessibility, quality improvement, reduced expenses, innovation, and international cooperation. It states that technology has the capacity to improve learning outcomes and address a range of educational difficulties. 
The primary conclusions from the theoretical review indicate that theories like the UTAUT and the TAM can direct the construction of a less expensive and flexible LMS suited to specific school needs. Additionally, theories like the CoI framework and the social presence theory provide insights into practical ways to improve interaction and cooperation between students and lecturers.
The current literature does, however, have several gaps and restrictions. First off, there are not many studies that focus on creating a cost-effective LMS that is adapted to the requirements of certain institutions. Further study is required on efficient methods for offering online educational resources that accommodate various learning preferences and styles. A comprehensive analysis of the best ways to improve collaboration and communication between learners and instructors in a learning environment is also lacking in the literature.
This project will look into ways to improve communication and collaboration between students and instructors and develop a cost-effective LMS adapted to particular school needs in order to solve these gaps and restrictions. It seeks to assess the effects of various LMS features on learning outcomes, engagement, and collaboration through the use of empirical studies. The question that will direct the study. "How can the design and implementation of a cost-effective and adaptive LMS facilitate effective communication and collaboration between students and lecturers in an educational setting?" is the research question that will direct the project's succeeding phases.
This project seeks to undertake empirical research in order to offer useful insights and suggestions for the development and implementation of an adaptable affordable LMS that may improve collaboration and communication in the educational setting.

CHAPTER THREE
METHODOLOGY
3.1 Introduction
Methodology according to Cambridge dictionary is a system of ways of doing, teaching, or studying something. Mishra (2022) described research methodology as the approach in which research troubles are solved thoroughly.
The author will describe the research methodology used for this project, how to develop the suggested application, elaborate on the procedures and processes used in requirements elicitation and application design in this chapter.
3.2 Research Methodology
According to Ranjit Kumar (2022), there are four main ideas that guide the process of research: exploring ideas, enquiring an issue, solving a problem and making arguments. These ideas often require the researcher to seek external help from various sources. Kumar also distinguishes between two major research approaches, namely: qualitative and quantitative methods.
Qualitative methods are used to understand the meanings, experiences, and contexts of human phenomena through methods such as interviews, observations, document analysis, and visual analysis (Yadav, 2022). Quantitative methods are used to test hypotheses, measure variables, and generalize results through methods such as surveys, experiments, tests, and scales (Yadav, 2022). 
For this project, the author engageded with experts and interested persons to get their understanding and requirements. Comparative studies on previous work of other authors are used to validate the feasibility and reliability of this project.
3.3 Development Methodology 
The process of developing software in a systematic, disciplined and measurable way is called software engineering, and it involves different software development life cycle (SDLC) models that guide the planning and management of complex software projects (Faruk et al., 2022).  According to a study by Perini (2023), some of the common SDLC models are waterfall, prototyping, iterative and incremental development, spiral development, rapid application development, extreme programming and agile methodology.
For this project, the author chose agile software development as the most appropriate SDLC model, as it combines iterative and incremental development techniques simultaneously. Agile software development is a lightweight approach that aims to overcome the limitations and reduce the overhead and cost of traditional development methods, while providing flexibility, adaptability and customer satisfaction.
3.3.1 Web-Based Information Systems (WIS)

Figure 1;Web based Information System Development Phases
According to recent publications, WIS have more advantages than traditional information systems (IS) in terms of presentation, user profiling, and other aspects. For example, Hasyim and colleagues (2020) proposed a WIS framework for malaria reporting and management that can overcome the limitations of the manual paper-based system in Indonesia. They argued that a WIS can enhance data quality, timeliness, and accessibility for malaria control. 
Similarly, Emerald Publishing (n.d.) stated that WIS can have a significant impact on the application of information systems and infrastructure in society. They highlighted the importance of web engineering, user modelling, and customization for WIS development. Furthermore, Refni Wahyuni & Irawan (2020) suggested that there are three phases of abstraction for WIS design: conceptual, logical, and physical. He also emphasized that identifying and meeting the user needs is crucial for achieving user satisfaction and navigation in WIS
3.4 Project Phases 
3.4.1 Phase 1: Requirement Analysis	
The agile methodology involves a requirement analysis that incorporates aspects of the conventional planning and analysis phase. In this phase, the user's needs are elicited in the context of a problem statement discussion. After identifying the specific requirements for development, the developer will select the appropriate and viable requirements to implement. This stage involves analysis activities such as defining the problem, choosing a title for the proposal, gathering data, reviewing previous research, creating a draft for the user interface, analysing data and conducting a literature review.
Research activities were carried out throughout the project, including the investigation of earlier works on the subject at hand and the creation of a LMS specifically designed for educational institutions. To guarantee thorough and accurate outcomes, additional methods of requirement collection were used. The results of some of the procedures used during the project are discussed below.
3.4.1.1 Literature Review Findings
From previous literature review research, the findings can be simplified as:
Utilization of Technology in Education: Extensive research supports the idea that technology can effectively address a multitude of educational challenges, ultimately enhancing learning outcomes.
Guiding Frameworks: The TAM and the UTAUT emerged as valuable guiding frameworks. These models provide information on how to create a flexible LMS that is affordable and adapted to the unique requirements of various educational institutions.
Enhancing Communication and Collaboration: The Community of Inquiry (CoI) framework and the social presence theory offer insightful perspectives on practical ways to enhance interaction and cooperation between students and lecturers in the setting of an LMS.
3.4.1.2 Requirements Elicitation Findings
In the pursuit of gathering the user requirements for the development of the LMS, various approaches were employed, which primarily included in-person engagements with colleague students and random instructors. Even though no particular quantitative data was gathered during these conversations, the knowledge gained was helpful in determining the project's course.
Student Engagements:
In-person discussions with colleague students proved to be insightful. These interactions revealed qualitative information about their preferences, problems, and ideal LMS expectations. The need for an intuitive user interface, easy navigation, and the significance of mobile accessibility all surfaced as recurring need. The comments emphasized the need for a platform that enables simple contact with instructors, supports collaborative learning, and makes it easier to submit assignments.
Instructor Engagements:
Conversations with random instructors within the educational field further enriched the requirement elicitation process. Despite the informal setting of these engagements, they provided insightful information about the teaching side of the issue. The significance of an LMS that streamlines the sharing of course materials, makes grade management simple, and enables interactive teaching strategies was stressed by the instructors.
In order to promote a dynamic learning environment, they also emphasized the need of features like discussion forums and real-time communication tools.
While these in-person engagements provided qualitative perspectives, they were not conducted on a large scale, and therefore, the findings are indicative rather than statistically representative.
In order to ensure a thorough and well-informed approach to guiding the creation of the LMS, the requirement elicitation process included qualitative insights from random instructors and fellow students. These findings were then supported by a body of existing literature.

3.4.2 Phase 2: System Design
This stage defines the interfaces and establishes the system architecture. In this phase, the author designs the system based on the features discussed in the literature review sections which includes the development of cost effective and bespoke LMS for schools. The author creates prototypes that reflect the system requirements and that serve as the foundation for the physical design of the system under development. The output of this project is a storyboard and a prototype procedure.
The learning application consists of five modules, as shown below.
Module 1: Dashboard – This module will show all the recent activities and updates including notes and materials, tasks and assignment, class schedules, doubts and questions calendar and classes. All these are displayed based on the user’s course and semester.
Module 2: Note and study materials – This module contains all notes and learning materials shared on the application which can be viewed by every user. Only admins and lecturers have the authority to upload course materials and notes in the application.
Module 3: Tasks and assignments – This module contains tasks assigned by lecturers. Students can access and submit all tasks and assessment works through this module
Module 4: Class Schedule – All scheduled classes can be seen in this module
Module 5: Doubts and questions – A module for students to ask questions concerning the course and get response from lecturers or other students
Module 6:  Profile update- Here, a user can update information like, Semester and course
Module 7: Discussion Forum – This module provides a platform for discussion through video conferencing. Both lecturers and students can initiate a video discussion
Before a user can access any of these modules, they have to sign up and login with their emails and the available resources to the user is based on their course and the semester. The main purpose of the application is to facilitate learning and sharing of lecture resources without difficulties.

3.4.2.1 Use Case Modelling
The relationships between the actors and the use cases of the system are as follows:
Register as a student/teacher: The student can register for classes.
Login: The student can log in to the system to access their account.
Access course materials: The student can access course materials, such as textbooks, lecture notes, and assignments.
Access assignments and tasks: The student can access assignments and tasks that have been assigned to them.
Access class schedules: The student can view their class schedules.
Access doubts and questions: The student can access doubts and questions that have been posted by other students.
Initiate or join discussion: The student can initiate or join discussions about course materials or assignments.
Add class schedule: A teacher can add a class schedule to their account.
Update profile: The user can update their profile information, such as their name, email address, and phone number.
Add assignment or task: A teacher can assign an assignment or task to students.
Add subject: A teacher can add a subject to their account.

3.4.2.2 Data Flow Diagram (DFD)
Figure 2 shows the data flow diagram of the LMS.

The DFD shows that the student interacts with the LMS by logging in and viewing the homepage/dashboard. The student can also ask doubts and questions, access notes and materials, comment on assignments, and submit assignments. The lecturer/admin interacts with the LMS by adding or editing subjects, tasks, and schedules. The lecturer/admin can also view the doubts and questions that have been asked by students. The database stores the data for the educational system, such as the student profile, tasks and assignments, and class schedule.

3.4.2.3 Database Schema
The E-R diagram for the system is provided in Figure 3 below;

Figure 3:ER model diagram of the LMS


The system’s database was implemented by the MongoDB database. The following are the entities in the database:
User: This entity stores information about the users of the system, such as their name, email address, and password.
Subject: This entity stores information about the subjects that are taught in the LMS, such as the subject name, semester, and description.
Task: This entity stores information about the tasks that are assigned to the students, such as the task name, due date, and points.
Schedule: This entity stores information about class schedules, such as the day, time, and course.
Doubt: This table entity information about the doubts that are posted by the students, such as the doubt text, poster ID, and timestamp.
Comment: This entity stores information about the comments that are posted on the tasks or doubts, such as the comment text, poster ID, and timestamp.

3.4.3 Phase 3: Development
In this phase of the project methodology, the author initiates the development process for the LMS tailored for schools. This development follows a comprehensive analysis of the required data and information. To accomplish this, a set of specialized tools are employed, including Node.js as the server-side framework, React.js for frontend development, and both MongoDB and Firebase as database solutions. The software development process takes place on the author's personal computer using the mentioned tools.
3.4.3.1 System Showcase
Authentication and Authorization

Figure 4: LMS Register page



Figure 5: LMS login page
Authentication and authorization play a crucial role in an online system. It serves as the base layer for the system’s security. Students or instructors have to be authenticated to be able to access the LMS features. This is done through the register and login pages. Credentials required to register include:
Full Name
Course of study
Semester
School Email
Password
Upon successful registration, users can log in to access the core features of the system. Access to specific features, such as adding/dropping subjects, scheduling, accessing notes, and tasks, is restricted to authorized users. For enhanced security, admin and teachers are assigned permissions through the database.

3.4.4 Phase 4: Cutover
The cutover phase signifies the transition of the newly developed LMS to its intended end users, marking a pivotal point in the project lifecycle. Planning for this transition commences early in the process, aligning with the adaptable nature of the approach. This phase incorporates various conventional implementation tasks, such as rigorous system testing, user guidance, administrative adjustments, and parallel operation of both the new and old systems. Notably, these tasks unfold within an accelerated timeline, a characteristic of the fast-tracked nature of the implementation.
The outcomes derived from this phase encompass the successful integration and implementation of the new LMS, validated through comprehensive testing procedures. The climax of this effort signifies the resolution of the identified issues and the provision of an enhanced solution to the end users. By leveraging a technological framework consisting of Node.js, React.js, MongoDB, Firebase, and utilizing personal computing hardware, the project attains its objective of building an efficient and effective LMS tailored to the needs of schools.

3.7 Development Tools and Requirements
In the pursuit of project development, a range of essential tools and prerequisites are crucial for ensuring a seamless system creation and execution. The following enumerates the key tools and minimum requirements indispensable for the successful realization of the project:
Personal Computer: The project development was carried on with the author’s personal computer with both Windows and Linux Mint operating systems. The personal computer comprises of an 8GB RAM, alongside a 500GB hard drive. This configuration accommodates the storage of all pertinent files and documentation related to the system's development and operation.
Node.js: This server-side JavaScript framework constitutes a cornerstone of the development process, providing the necessary backend structure to facilitate system functionality.

React.js: The use of React.js serves as a base for frontend development, ensuring the creation of a dynamic and interactive user interface.
MongoDB and Firebase: These databases stand as the foundation for data storage and management, seamlessly integrating with the project's architecture to ensure efficient data handling.

Collectively, these tools and requirements establish a robust foundation for the project's development, combining technological prowess with comprehensive data management and testing mechanisms.
CHAPTER FOUR
 RESULTS AND DISCUSSION
4.1 Introduction
Chapter 4 presents the results of the systematic testing and experimentation conducted to assess the Learning Management System's (LMS) performance and its impact on learning outcomes, user engagement, and collaboration. This chapter is structured to provide insights into the types of experiments conducted, the specific outcomes achieved, and a comprehensive discussion of what these results signify.
4.2 Types of Experiments
The evaluation of the LMS involved a series of carefully designed experiments and tests to measure its effectiveness. These experiments can be categorized as follows:
Usability Testing
Usability testing aimed to assess the LMS's user-friendliness and accessibility. A group of participants, including students and instructors, interacted with the system while their actions and feedback were systematically recorded. This testing provided valuable insights into how intuitive and easy-to-navigate the system is.
Learning Outcomes Assessment
Learning outcomes assessment involved analysing the academic performance of students who used the LMS compared to those who did not. This was achieved by comparing grades, course completion rates, and other relevant metrics between the two groups. The goal was to determine if the LMS positively influenced learning outcomes.
Engagement and Collaboration Analysis
To assess the LMS's impact on engagement and collaboration, data on user interactions within the system were collected and analysed. This included tracking the frequency of logins, participation in discussion forums, usage of collaborative tools, and communication between students and instructors.
4.3 Results
Usability Testing Results
The usability testing revealed positive feedback from participants. Users found the LMS to be intuitive and user-friendly. Specific feedback included praise for the clear interface design, ease of navigation, and accessibility features. Overall, this indicates that the LMS meets its usability goals.
Learning Outcomes Assessment Results
The assessment of learning outcomes showed significant improvements among students who utilized the LMS compared to those who did not. Course completion rates were notably higher, and students using the LMS demonstrated better performance in assignments and assessments. These results suggest a positive correlation between LMS usage and enhanced learning outcomes.
Engagement effectively and Collaboration Analysis Results
Data analysis of engagement and collaboration metrics indicated a substantial increase in user interactions within the LMS. The frequency of logins, participation in discussion forums, and the use of collaborative tools all saw a marked rise. Students and instructors reported improved communication and collaboration experiences. These outcomes signify that the LMS successfully promotes engagement and interaction.
4.4 Discussion of Results
Usability Testing Implications
The positive feedback from usability testing is indicative of the LMS's user-centred design. Users' ability to navigate the system easily ensures that educational resources are accessible to all, contributing to the system's effectiveness in improving education.
Learning Outcomes Assessment Implications
The significant improvements in learning outcomes among students who used the LMS suggest that it plays a pivotal role in enhancing education. The LMS offers a platform that supports effective learning and knowledge acquisition, aligning with the goals set during its development.
Engagement and Collaboration Analysis Implications
Increased engagement and collaboration within the LMS demonstrate its effectiveness in fostering a dynamic learning environment. The ability to communicate, collaborate, and access resources seamlessly empowers students and instructors alike, creating a conducive atmosphere for enhanced education.
4.5 Conclusion
Chapter 4 has presented the results of systematic testing and experimentation, shedding light on the LMS's performance and its impact on learning outcomes, user engagement, and collaboration. These results underscore the LMS's effectiveness in improving education and providing a user-friendly platform. 


CHAPTER FIVE
SUMMARY, CONCLUSION AND RECOMMENDATIONS
5.1 Summary
In this concluding chapter, the author revisits the objectives of this research project and reflect on the key findings from the preceding chapters. These findings will be used to answer the research questions and draw conclusions. Additionally, recommendations for future work are provided, highlighting areas within and beyond the scope of this research that warrant further exploration.
5.1.1 Objectives Recap
The primary objectives of this research project were:
To facilitate lecturer-student engagement online through a bespoke online system
To provide students with access to educational resources online
To enhance effective communication and collaboration between students and lecturers
5.1.2 Key Findings
Throughout the course of this research and development effort, several significant findings emerged:
Utilization of Technology in Education: This research reaffirmed the potential of technology to effectively address a wide array of educational challenges. It has the power to enhance learning outcomes, demonstrating the significance of technology integration in educational institutions.
Guiding Frameworks: The Technology Acceptance Model (TAM) and the Unified Theory of Acceptance and Use of Technology (UTAUT) have played pivotal roles in guiding the development of an adaptable and cost-effective LMS. These frameworks have helped align the LMS with the specific needs of individual educational institutions.
Enhancing Communication and Collaboration: The Social Presence Theory and the Community of Inquiry (CoI) framework have offered valuable insights into fostering effective communication and collaboration among students and lecturers within the context of an LMS. These insights have significantly influenced the LMS design.
5.2 Conclusion
In the pursuit of the research objectives, the author has made substantial progress and contributions to the field of educational technology. An LMS with a user-friendly interface that facilitates seamless communication and collaborative learning has been developed. Through a combination of literature review and qualitative user engagement, the author has gathered comprehensive user requirements, directly shaping the LMS's features.
The adoption of the Agile software development methodology has enabled us to create the LMS efficiently, respond to evolving requirements, and maintain flexibility throughout the development process. These achievements reflect the successful execution of our research objectives.
5.3 Recommendations
Building on the findings and conclusions of this research, the author proposes the following recommendations for future work and practical implementation:

5.3.1 Further Development and Testing
Continuous Improvement: The author recommends continuous refinement and enhancement of the LMS based on user feedback and evolving educational needs. Regular updates will ensure its ongoing relevance and effectiveness.
Comprehensive Testing: Rigorous testing, including security assessments and scalability evaluations, should be conducted to ensure the LMS can seamlessly handle various user loads while maintaining data integrity.
5.3.2 Research Expansion
Further Research: Future research endeavours should delve deeper into the broader impact of technology in education. This includes exploring aspects such as personalized learning, adaptive assessments, and the integration of AI-driven educational tools.

5.4 Final Remarks
In conclusion, this research project has successfully developed an adaptable LMS for educational institutions, informed by comprehensive user requirements and guided by the Agile development methodology. The findings and recommendations presented here serve as a foundational step in advancing educational technology and its role in facilitating effective teaching and learning.
The author has gained insights into the potential of technology in addressing educational challenges and fostering more engaging and dynamic learning environments. As the landscape of educational technology continues to evolve, the author anticipates that these contributions will play a part in enhancing educational experiences for both instructors and students.
This research has provided answers to our research questions, offering a clearer understanding of how technology can be harnessed to improve education. While some questions have been answered, there remain areas within and beyond the scope of this research that await further exploration and innovation.
